region_of_origin: Singapore (served); chai spice tradition originates in South Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for iced chai tea latte; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for spiced milk tea does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code exists for iced chai latte with milk in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for iced chai tea latte identified in the SNOMED CT International Edition as of 2024.

main_ingredients: Chai spice concentrate (cardamom, cinnamon, ginger, black pepper, clove)

serving.natural_language_portion: One Grande-size serving (approximately 473 g / 16 fl oz) is the standard commercial portion for an iced chai tea latte, equivalent to a large cup as served by major café chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chai Tea Latte, Iced (SR Legacy / Branded Foods composite); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for milk-tea beverage category
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 50.74 kcal, protein 1.69 g, fat 1.48 g, carbohydrate 7.82 g, sugar 7.61 g, sodium 23.26 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, calcium 63.42 mg, iron 0.04 mg, cholesterol 5.29 mg, saturated fat 0.95 g, trans fat 0.06 g, vitamin C 0.0 mg) are internally consistent and plausible for a sweetened milk-based chai tea beverage. Values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central entries for iced chai latte (e.g., FDC ID 2103689, Starbucks Iced Chai Tea Latte) and retained as the best available estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are not reported in the input and are set to -1 (data not available).

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea / chai latte category; University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk-based tea beverages.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for iced chai tea latte exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food category: sweetened milk-based tea beverages and chai latte, which range from GI 45–65 depending on sugar content and milk type. A midpoint of 55 is used, consistent with the 7.61 g/100 g sugar content and dairy milk base. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of exactly 55 falls in the low category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 41.6) / 100 = 22.9, using the full 473 g serving carbohydrate content of 41.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (7.61 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 23.26 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit sweetened chai tea lattes because the 7.61 g/100 g sugar load (35.9 g per Grande serving) independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines on sugar-sweetened beverages, even though sodium content is low; request an unsweetened version with no added sugar or chai syrup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (7.61 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and low-GI boundary value (55) combine to produce a high glycemic load of approximately 22.9 per Grande serving, causing significant postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this sweetened iced chai tea latte; the 35.9 g of sugar per Grande serving with zero fibre and a glycemic load of ~22.9 will cause significant postprandial hyperglycaemia — if chai flavour is desired, prepare unsweetened homemade chai with cinnamon, ginger, cardamom, and clove in unsweetened milk to access spice benefits without the glycaemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (0.95 g/100 g; 4.5 g per Grande serving) and trans fat (0.06 g/100 g; 0.28 g per Grande serving) from dairy milk, combined with high added sugar, contribute to cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to its combination of added sugar (7.61 g/100 g), saturated fat (0.95 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.06 g/100 g), all of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased CVD risk; choose an unsweetened version made with low-fat or plant-based milk to reduce saturated fat and sugar exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (7.61 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, outweighing the anti-inflammatory benefit of chai spices in this commercial sweetened form.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Although chai spices (cinnamon, ginger, cardamom, clove) have documented anti-inflammatory properties, the high added sugar in commercial iced chai latte promotes inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; prepare unsweetened homemade chai with fresh spices in milk to access anti-inflammatory benefits without the sugar-driven inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Chai Tea Latte is a chilled, sweetened milk-based beverage made from a blend of black tea and traditional South Asian spices — primarily cardamom, cinnamon, ginger, black pepper, and clove — combined with dairy milk and typically sweetened with sugar or a commercial chai concentrate, then poured over ice. It is widely served in café chains across Singapore and Southeast Asia, often in large portions (Grande: 473 g / 16 fl oz). The flavour profile is aromatic, warmly spiced, mildly sweet, and creamy, with a light tea astringency balanced by the richness of milk. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 50.7 kcal, 1.69 g protein, 1.48 g fat (0.95 g saturated), 7.82 g carbohydrate, and 7.61 g sugar, with zero dietary fibre and low sodium (23.26 mg). Calcium content is notable at 63.42 mg/100 g from the dairy milk base. The high sugar content (7.61 g/100 g) and zero fibre make this beverage a concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 55 (low GI boundary) and glycemic load of approximately 22.9 per Grande serving indicate significant postprandial glucose impact despite the low GI classification. Chai spices have documented anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are largely negated in the commercial sweetened form. Vegetarian and gluten-free; contains dairy. Common in Western-style café menus in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is iced chai tea latte healthy?
How much sugar is in a chai latte?
Can diabetics drink chai tea latte?
Is chai latte good for high blood pressure?
Calories in iced chai latte Grande
What spices are in chai tea latte?
Is iced chai latte suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values are internally consistent and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central branded entries for iced chai tea latte (e.g., Starbucks Iced Chai Tea Latte, FDC ID 2103689). The input source (GrabFood / AI-generated) is a forbidden primary source, so values are treated as an adapted estimate anchored to USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are not available from the input and are set to -1. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, mineral panel, and moisture data for this commercial iced chai tea latte preparation are not available in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy database or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. VITAMIN C: vitamin_c_mg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero / trace) — consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for milk-tea beverages; chai spices contribute negligible ascorbic acid after dilution. 3. DIETARY FIBRE: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) — the beverage is a liquid with no intact plant cell-wall material; consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for similar beverages. 4. SOURCE ADAPTATION: The original input listed GrabFood as the data source, which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2. Per-100g values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 2103689, Starbucks Iced Chai Tea Latte) and retained as plausible. 5. GI CLASSIFICATION CORRECTION (Iteration 3): Previous draft incorrectly assigned gi_category 'medium' for glycemic_index 55. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of exactly 55 falls in the LOW category. gi_category has been corrected to 'low'. The glycemic load of 22.9 per Grande serving remains high despite the low GI classification, due to the large serving size (473 g) and 41.6 g total carbohydrate per serving. 6. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for sweetened milk-based tea beverages; no direct GI measurement for iced chai tea latte exists in the database as of 2024. 7. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: This beverage is classified as Western cuisine served in Singapore; Singaporean multilingual naming rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are applied. Chinese name 冰印度奶茶 (Bīng Yìndù Nǎichá, literally 'iced Indian milk tea') is the most common Chinese-language reference for this beverage category in Singapore. 8. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g of 0.06 g/100 g is retained from input; this is consistent with naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) present in dairy milk at low levels. 9. ALIASES CONSISTENCY CHECK: All strings in aliases_by_language values verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Duplicate '冰印度奶茶' entry removed from previous draft's flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.
Iteration 2: Verified gi_category against glycemic_index value; previous draft incorrectly set gi_category to 'medium' for GI 55.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the standard international GI classification scale. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect corrected classification. Removed duplicate alias entry. Updated notes section with GI classification correction documentation.
